(abbr. **NWFP**), commonly known as ***Sarhad*** (lit. 'Frontier'), was a [province of Pakistan](/wiki/Administrative_units_of_Pakistan "Administrative units of Pakistan") from 1947 to 1955 and from 1970 to 2010; and prior, a [province of British Raj](/wiki/Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India "Presidencies and provinces of British India") from [its establishment](#Formation) in 1901 to [Pakistan's independence](/wiki/Pakistan_Movement "Pakistan Movement") in 1947. It was established from the north-western districts of [British Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)") during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").[[1]](#cite_note-4) Following the [referendum in 1947 to join either Pakistan or India](/wiki/1947_North-West_Frontier_Province_referendum "1947 North-West Frontier Province referendum"), the province voted hugely in favour of joining [Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan") and it acceded accordingly on 14 August 1947. It was dissolved to form a unified province of [West Pakistan](/wiki/West_Pakistan "West Pakistan") in 1955 upon promulgation of [One Unit Scheme](/wiki/One_Unit_Scheme "One Unit Scheme") and was reestablished in [1970](/wiki/Legal_Framework_Order,_1970 "Legal Framework Order, 1970"). It was known by this name until 19 April 2010, when it was dissolved and redesignated as the province of [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") following the enactment of the [Eighteenth Amendment](/wiki/Eighteenth_Amendment_to_the_Constitution_of_Pakistan "Eighteenth Amendment to the Constitution of Pakistan") to the [Constitution of Pakistan](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan "Constitution of Pakistan").

The **North-West Frontier Province** (abbr. **NWFP**), commonly known as ***Sarhad*** (lit. 'Frontier'), was a [province of Pakistan](/wiki/Administrative_units_of_Pakistan "Administrative units of Pakistan") from 1947 to 1955 and from 1970 to 2010; and prior, a [province of British Raj](/wiki/Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India "Presidencies and provinces of British India") from [its establishment](#Formation) in 1901 to [Pakistan's independence](/wiki/Pakistan_Movement "Pakistan Movement") in 1947. It was established from the north-western districts of [British Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)") during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").[[1]](#cite_note-4) Following the [referendum in 1947 to join either Pakistan or India](/wiki/1947_North-West_Frontier_Province_referendum "1947 North-West Frontier Province referendum"), the province voted hugely in favour of joining [Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan") and it acceded accordingly on 14 August 1947. It was dissolved to form a unified province of [West Pakistan](/wiki/West_Pakistan "West Pakistan") in 1955 upon promulgation of [One Unit Scheme](/wiki/One_Unit_Scheme "One Unit Scheme") and was reestablished in [1970](/wiki/Legal_Framework_Order,_1970 "Legal Framework Order, 1970"). It was known by this name until 19 April 2010, when it was dissolved and redesignated as the province of [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") following the enactment of the [Eighteenth Amendment](/wiki/Eighteenth_Amendment_to_the_Constitution_of_Pakistan "Eighteenth Amendment to the Constitution of Pakistan") to the [Constitution of Pakistan](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan "Constitution of Pakistan").

The province covered an area of 70,709 km2 (27,301 sq mi), including much of the current Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province but excluding the [Federally Administered Tribal Areas](/wiki/Federally_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Federally Administered Tribal Areas") and the former [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") of [Amb](/wiki/Amb_(princely_state) "Amb (princely state)"), [Chitral](/wiki/Chitral_(princely_state) "Chitral (princely state)"), [Dir](/wiki/Dir_(princely_state) "Dir (princely state)"), [Phulra](/wiki/Phulra "Phulra") and [Swat](/wiki/Swat_(princely_state) "Swat (princely state)"). Its capital was the city of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), and the province was composed of six [divisions](/wiki/Divisions_of_Pakistan "Divisions of Pakistan") ([Bannu](/wiki/Bannu_Division "Bannu Division"), [Dera Ismail Khan](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan_Division "Dera Ismail Khan Division"), [Hazara](/wiki/Hazara_Division "Hazara Division"), [Kohat](/wiki/Kohat_Division "Kohat Division"), [Mardan](/wiki/Mardan_Division "Mardan Division"), and [Peshawar Division](/wiki/Peshawar_Division "Peshawar Division"); [Malakand](/wiki/Malakand_Division "Malakand Division") was later added as the seventh division). Until 1947, the province was bordered by five princely states to the north, the minor states of the [Gilgit Agency](/wiki/Gilgit_Agency "Gilgit Agency") to the northeast, the province of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)") to the east and the province of [Balochistan](/wiki/Baluchistan_(Chief_Commissioner%27s_Province) "Baluchistan (Chief Commissioner's Province)") to the south. The [Kingdom of Afghanistan](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Afghanistan "Kingdom of Afghanistan") lay to the northwest, with the Federally Administered Tribal Areas forming a buffer zone between the two.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North-West_Frontier_Province&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North-West_Frontier_Province&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation")]

See also: [Military history of the North-West Frontier](/wiki/Military_history_of_the_North-West_Frontier "Military history of the North-West Frontier")

The northwestern frontier areas were annexed by the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") after the [Second Sikh War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Sikh_War "Second Anglo-Sikh War") (1848–49). The territories thenceforth formed a part of Punjab until the province, then known as North West Frontier Province, was created in 1901 from the north-western districts of the Punjab Province.[[2]](#cite_note-5)
This region, along with the 'Frontier Tribal Areas', acted as a [buffer zone](/wiki/Buffer_zone "Buffer zone") with Afghanistan.

### Inside Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North-West_Frontier_Province&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Inside Pakistan")]

Before the [Partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India"), the [1947 North-West Frontier Province referendum](/wiki/1947_North-West_Frontier_Province_referendum "1947 North-West Frontier Province referendum") was held in July 1947 to decide the future of NWFP, in which the people of the province decided in favor of joining Pakistan. [Chief Minister](/wiki/List_of_Chief_Ministers_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "List of Chief Ministers of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") [Dr Khan Sahib](/wiki/Khan_Abdul_Jabbar_Khan "Khan Abdul Jabbar Khan"), along with his brother [Bacha Khan](/wiki/Bacha_Khan "Bacha Khan") and the [Khudai Khidmatgars](/wiki/Khudai_Khidmatgar "Khudai Khidmatgar"), boycotted the referendum, citing that it did not have the options of the NWFP becoming [independent](/wiki/Pashtunistan "Pashtunistan") or joining Afghanistan.[[3]](#cite_note-6)[[4]](#cite_note-7)

As a separate province, the NWFP lasted until 1955 when it was merged into the new province of [West Pakistan](/wiki/West_Pakistan "West Pakistan"), under the [One Unit](/wiki/One_Unit "One Unit") policy announced by [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Pakistan "Prime Minister of Pakistan") [Chaudhry Mohammad Ali](/wiki/Chaudhry_Mohammad_Ali "Chaudhry Mohammad Ali"). It was recreated after the dissolution of the One Unit system and lasted under its old nomenclature until April 2010, when it was renamed the [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") province.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North-West_Frontier_Province&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Population")]

Historical population, language, and religious counts in North-West Frontier Province were enumerated in all districts ([Hazara](/wiki/Hazara_District "Hazara District"), [Mardan](/wiki/Mardan_District "Mardan District"), [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar_District "Peshawar District"), [Kohat](/wiki/Kohat_District "Kohat District"), [Bannu](/wiki/Bannu_District "Bannu District"), and [Dera Ismail Khan](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan_District "Dera Ismail Khan District")), detailed in the population, language, and religious tables above and below. Separate population counts were taken in the [Agencies and Tribal Areas](/wiki/Federally_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Federally Administered Tribal Areas"), as detailed on the respective article page.

At independence, there was a clear Muslim [Pashtun](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns"), [Hindkowan](/wiki/Hindkowans "Hindkowans"), and [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabis "Punjabis") majority in the North-West Frontier Province, although there were also significant Hindu and Sikh [Pashtun](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns"), [Hindkowan](/wiki/Hindkowans "Hindkowans"), and [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabis "Punjabis") minorities scattered across the province.

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North-West_Frontier_Province&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Language")]

The languages of the North-West Frontier Province included Pashto, Punjabi, Kohistani and others, although most of the population spoke either [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language") or [Western Punjabi](/wiki/Lahnda "Lahnda") (primarily [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko") and [Saraiki](/wiki/Saraiki_language "Saraiki language")). Prior to the arrival of the British, the *[official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language")*, for governmental uses and such, was [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language").

Adherents of Islam who were indigenous to frontier regions that continued to have relatively large Hindu populations, and who were also relatively recent [converts](/wiki/Conversion_to_Islam "Conversion to Islam"), were influenced by some traditions of Hinduism; in contrast, Muslims in frontier regions that had been further influenced by [orthodox Islam](/wiki/Orthodox_Islam "Orthodox Islam") and converted at a much earlier date were noted in their relatively different cultural habits.

> *"The high road, along which the [Mohammedan](/wiki/Mohammedan "Mohammedan") [conquerors and rulers of India](/wiki/Muslim_conquests_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Muslim conquests in the Indian subcontinent") passed and repassed lay through the north (the [Khyber](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass"), [Kurram](/wiki/Kurram_District "Kurram District") and other routes); and it is probable that Islam never took so firm a hold of the inhabitants of the southern district as of the people to the north of them. In this connection it is interesting to notice that the [Mussalman](/wiki/Mussalman "Mussalman") of the [Derajat](/wiki/Derajat "Derajat") is less strict in his observance of the duties of his religion, such as fasts, prayers and the like, than his northern neighbours. Through [Hazara](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region") lay the road by which the Emperors of [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi") went to and fro between the capital and their summer retreat in [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir"), and it was natural that Islam should thoroughly permeate the district. Similarly [Kohat](/wiki/Kohat_District "Kohat District"), from its situation with regard to the [Kurram Valley](/wiki/Kurram_Valley "Kurram Valley"), which at no very distant period was, nominally at least, a portion of the [Afghan kingdom](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire"), has been more influenced in the past by its [Mohammedan](/wiki/Mohammedan "Mohammedan") neighbours to the west than have the [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_British_India "Districts of British India") to the south of it. There is no need to consider here the probable date at which the bulk of the [Pathans](/wiki/Pathans "Pathans") living in the Province, or rather their ancestors, were [converted to Islam](/wiki/Converted_to_Islam "Converted to Islam"). It is enough to notice that they had long been Mohammadan when they settled in their present homes, and that their [fanaticism](/wiki/Fanaticism "Fanaticism") and intolerance, especially in the districts where they are strongest, rendered the Province no very inviting place of residence for settlers of a different creed. If no fanaticism in its inhabitants acted as a bar to the settlement of [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") in [Hazara](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region"), the absence of any large [trade](/wiki/Trade "Trade") centres was at least equally efficacious. The only other district in which there is a non-Pathan element in the population in any way commensurate to that of Hazara is [Dera Ismail Khan](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan_District "Dera Ismail Khan District"). The population here is mainly composed of tribes of Indian origin. Its conversion to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is of much later data; fanaticism does not exist, and no particular dislike to the Hindu seems to have existed."* [[10]](#cite_note-NWFP1911-13): 64–65

— Excerpt from the [Census of India](/wiki/Census_of_India "Census of India") (North-West Frontier Province), 1911 AD

Similarly, adherents of Hinduism who belonged to the various [castes](/wiki/Caste "Caste") and [tribes](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe") who were [indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") to the frontier regions had considerable Islamic influence, owing to their status as a [religious minority](/wiki/Religious_minority "Religious minority") in the region for centuries, and thus formed [religious syncretism](/wiki/Religious_syncretism "Religious syncretism") that incorporated aspects from both faiths into their cultures and traditions.

> *"[Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), as it exists in the North-West Frontier Province, is but a pale reflection of the system which flourishes in the [United Provinces](/wiki/United_Provinces_(1937%E2%80%931950) "United Provinces (1937–1950)") and other areas to the east. Even of the [Derajat](/wiki/Derajat "Derajat"), where, as we have seen, the [Hindu](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") population is proportionately most numerous, the writer of the [Dera Ismail Khan](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan_District "Dera Ismail Khan District") [Gazetteer](/wiki/Gazetteer "Gazetteer") notes, "the [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") of this district are less particular in the matter of caste prejudices and observances than down country Hindus. Most of them will drink water that has been carried in Mussaks (skins for carrying water) or out of [lotas](/wiki/Lota_(vessel) "Lota (vessel)") detached from a working [well](/wiki/Well "Well"). They habitually ride on [donkeys](/wiki/Donkey "Donkey") and do a multitude of other things which an [orthodox Hindu](/w/index.php?title=Orthodox_Hinduism&action=edit&redlink=1 "Orthodox Hinduism (page does not exist)") would shrink from. All [idolatrous](/wiki/Idolatrous "Idolatrous") observances are kept very much in the background. Except a few small images (thakurs) kept in their [mandirs](/wiki/Mandirs "Mandirs") they have no [idols](/wiki/Cult_image "Cult image") at all. Nor is it their habit to take their [gods](/wiki/Gods_in_Hinduism "Gods in Hinduism") about in [procession](/wiki/Procession "Procession"). No one, in fact, sees anything of their [worship](/wiki/Worship_in_Hinduism "Worship in Hinduism"). They [burn their dead](/wiki/Cremation "Cremation"), and throw the ashes into the [Indus](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River"). They always keep a few of the bones, and take them, when the opportunity offers, to the [Ganges](/wiki/Ganges "Ganges")... There are a good many [dharamsalas](/wiki/Dharamshala_(type_of_building) "Dharamshala (type of building)"), [mandirs](/wiki/Mandirs "Mandirs"), and dawaras at [Dera Ismail Khan](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan_Tehsil "Dera Ismail Khan Tehsil") and in the cis-[Indus](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") [tehsils](/wiki/Tehsil "Tehsil")."* [[10]](#cite_note-NWFP1911-13): 93

— Excerpt from the [Census of India](/wiki/Census_of_India "Census of India") (North-West Frontier Province), 1911 AD

Lastly, decadal census reports throughout the colonial era frequently detailed the difficulty of differentiating adherents of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") with adherents of [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), owing to the traditional ability of the former in [assimilating](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation") and integrating followers of varied thought into Hinduism.

> *"The [Sikh religion](/wiki/Sikh_religion "Sikh religion") was born out of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), and fears have been expressed of its being reabsorbed into it. Truly wonderful is the strength and [vitality](/wiki/Vitality "Vitality") of Hinduism. It is like the [boa constrictor](/wiki/Boa_constrictor "Boa constrictor") of the Indian forests; when a petty enemy appears to worry it, it winds round its opponent, crushes it in its folds, and finally causes it to disappear in its capacious interior. In this way, many centuries ago, [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") on its own ground disposed of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") which was largely a [Hindu reformation](/wiki/Hindu_reformation "Hindu reformation") in this way in a [prehistoric period](/wiki/History_of_India#Classical_period_(c._200_BCE_–_c._650_CE) "History of India") it absorbed the religion of the [Scythian invaders of Northern India](/wiki/Indo-Scythians "Indo-Scythians"); in this way it has converted [educated Islam in India](/wiki/Hindu%E2%80%93Islamic_relations#Similarities "Hindu–Islamic relations") into a semi-[paganism](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism"); and in this way it is disposing of the reformed and once hopeful religion of [Baba Nanak](/wiki/Baba_Nanak "Baba Nanak"). [Hinduism has embraced Sikhism](/wiki/Hinduism_and_Sikhism "Hinduism and Sikhism") in its folds; the still comparatively young religion is making a vigorous struggle for life, but its ultimate destruction is, it is apprehended, inevitable without State support. Notwithstanding the [Sikh Guru's](/wiki/Sikh_gurus "Sikh gurus") powerful denunciation of [Brahmans](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin"), [secular](/wiki/Secular "Secular") [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikhs "Sikhs") now rarely do anything without their assistance. [Brahmans](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") help them to be born, help them [to wed](/wiki/Anand_Karaj "Anand Karaj"), help them to die and help their souls after death to obtain a state of bliss. And Brahmans, with all the deftness of [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") [missionaries](/wiki/Missionaries "Missionaries") in [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") countries have partially succeeded in persuading the Sikhs to restore to their niches the images of [Devi](/wiki/Devi "Devi"), the Queen of Heaven, and the Saints and gods of the ancient faith."* [[10]](#cite_note-NWFP1911-13): 62

— Excerpt from the [Census of India](/wiki/Census_of_India "Census of India") (North-West Frontier Province), 1911 AD)
